evidence: goseg/config/wireguard.go defines package-level function-variable seams (`confForWG`, `getVersionChannelForWG`, `wgKeyGenForCycle`, `updateConfTypedForCycle`) and the tests in `goseg/config/wireguard_test.go` rebind them to alternate implementations.

goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go defines a shared contract constant `ProtocolVersion` and action parser/dispatch helpers, but there is no corresponding explicit consumer check at transport/message boundaries in the listed runtime files.
`ParseAction` in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` indexes a namespace map and iterates a potentially nil slice without a branch for unsupported namespaces.

Multiple files implement compatibility-style pass-through functions (`StartBroadcastSubsystem` -> `Initialize`, `ApplyBroadcastUpdate` -> `ApplyBroadcastTransition`, `callUpdater` -> `callUpdaterWithRuntime`, `updateDocker` -> `updateDockerWithRuntime`, `LoadWireguard` -> `loadWireguard(newWireguardRuntime())`, `WriteWgConf` -> `writeWgConfWithRuntime`).

The same seam pattern appears repeatedly in `broadcast`, `routines`, `shipworkflow`, and docker orchestration paths, creating deep call chains while still exposing the lower-level behavior in the same package.

Several runtime seams are effectively test-injected type wrappers around package-level singletons and one concrete default implementation, giving minimal policy surface for the added abstraction layer.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` and `goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go` both define overlapping runtime seam interfaces and default impl sets for the same domain operations (container lifecycle, config/state access, networking, command execution), creating duplicated abstraction layers.

Command/handler functions mix orchestration, I/O, and transport concerns in single functions (for example, `SupportHandlerWithRuntime` in `goseg/handler/system/support.go` and `runImportedPierWorkflow`/`extractUploadedPier` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`).

Long-running event handlers in `goseg/routines/docker.go` interleave state mutation, restart policy, and broadcast side effects in the same switch body, producing high coupling and repeated transition/update patterns.

findings.summary: Protocol version constant is a dead artifact and does not enforce cross-module compatibility at the action boundary.
evidence: `ProtocolVersion` is declared in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` with no references outside that file.

Upload action intake in `goseg/uploadsvc/adapters/upload_payload.go` validates only action token strings, and `goseg/uploadsvc/service.go` routes via `actions.ParseUploadAction` without any explicit protocol version check.
There is no version gate before dispatch and no compatibility fail-fast path at this boundary.
findings.suggestion: Either remove `ProtocolVersion` or enforce it in the websocket action intake path (e.g., include version in incoming payload/header, validate in `ParseAction`/adapter layer, and reject mismatched clients before dispatch) to keep protocol contract checks centralized.

findings.summary: An extra click-level response shim exists separately from the active runtime parser path, creating duplicated boundaries and a stale module entry point.
evidence: `goseg/click/filter.go` defines `filterResponse` as a one-line pass-through to `response.ParsePokeResponse` and retains commented legacy logic.
Production call paths in `goseg/click/restore/restore.go` depend on `runtime.FilterResponse`, not `filterResponse` in `goseg/click/filter.go`.
A live parser boundary already exists in `goseg/click/internal/runtime/runtime.go`, making `filterResponse` an additional unconsumed indirection layer.
findings.suggestion: Delete or retire `goseg/click/filter.go` and standardize on a single parser boundary (`runtime.FilterResponse` or another explicit shared abstraction), then remove the duplicate shim and commented legacy block.

findings.summary: Thin runtime wrappers add indirection without enforcing policy across broadcast/version/orchestration entrypoints.
evidence: `ApplyBroadcastUpdate` is a compatibility shim that only wraps `ApplyBroadcastTransition` with a `functionTransition` and no additional policy.
`callUpdater` and `updateDocker` in `routines/version.go` only forward to `*WithRuntime(newVersionRuntime(), ...)` variants.
`LoadWireguard` and `WriteWgConf` are one-line delegators to `wireguardRuntime` functions.
This same shape appears in multiple modules and contributes to a consistent `max_chain_depth=4` indirection profile reported in seam-heavy files.
findings.suggestion: Keep only one exported/public entrypoint per concrete behavior and drop compatibility aliases; keep runtime-override seams private-to-tests. For production paths, call the concrete implementation directly through a single seam function instead of repeated `A -> AWithRuntime` wrappers.

findings.summary: Runtime interfaces are over-declared with only default implementations, limiting interface leverage.
evidence: `runtime_seams.go` defines many interfaces (contextOps, containerOps, commandOps, etc.) each with only one default implementation used by construction.
`routines/runtime_seams.go` similarly defines multiple small interfaces (`stateOps`, `containerOps`, `broadcastOps`, etc.) where production always uses `*Default` structs.
`shipworkflow/startram.go` uses a function-bag `startramRuntime` with mostly direct package function bindings and no observable strategy selection in production.
The patterns are duplicated across modules, inflating maintenance surface while adding little polymorphic value.
findings.suggestion: For modules with no alternate production implementations, replace broad interface hierarchies with compact function-set structs or plain closure fields; reserve true interfaces only where multiple providers are expected and documented.

findings.summary: Global auth singleton is exposed as a mutable object, leaking policy boundaries through `GetClientManager()`.
evidence: `GetClientManager()` returns the package-global `*structs.ClientManager` directly with no interface abstraction.
`broadcast` and other callers invoke behavior by directly reaching through this pointer (`auth.GetClientManager().BroadcastAuth`, `auth.GetClientManager().HasAuthSession()`).
Shared mutable singleton escape makes callers depend on full concrete type instead of a small behavioral contract, reducing abstraction boundary clarity.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a narrow `AuthManager` interface (e.g., `HasAuthSession`, `BroadcastAuth`, `AddAuthClient`, `BroadcastUnauth`) and inject it into runtimes/handlers, keeping the concrete singleton private to `auth` package internals.

findings.summary: Config module uses mutable package-level function indirection seams that blur boundary contracts and invite hidden behavioral drift.
evidence: Runtime functions in `goseg/config/wireguard.go` depend on package-local vars (`confForWG`, `getVersionChannelForWG`, `wgKeyGenForCycle`, `updateConfTypedForCycle`) instead of direct dependencies.

`goseg/config/wireguard_test.go` resets and reassigns those vars to stub behavior in multiple tests, showing cross-file behavior is coupled to mutable package globals rather than explicit constructor/runtime seams.
findings.suggestion: Replace package-global function indirection with explicit dependency injection via constructor args or interfaces at the boundary, and keep package internals immutable in production; keep tests using dedicated test seams/fakes passed in through test-only constructors.

findings.summary: Unknown action namespaces are not explicitly classified, weakening contract enforcement at parser boundaries.
evidence: `ParseAction` in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` obtains allowed actions via map indexing and loops over the result without handling missing namespace keys before iteration.

The error path returns an unsupported action error with the provided action but no explicit namespace-rejection path, so invalid namespace inputs can collapse into an ambiguous unsupported-action signal.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit namespace validation branch in `ParseAction` and return a distinct namespace-level contract error (with namespace included) before action lookup, then update tests to assert this boundary behavior.

findings.summary: Protocol action version is declared as a contract constant but is not enforced at module boundaries, creating docs/runtime drift.
evidence: The protocol version constant is defined in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` but only declared there; listed runtime/adaptation files do not consume it for validation.

`README.md` describes the websocket contract version, yet enforcement point in the request-adaptation/dispatch path (`upload_payload_test.go` scope) is token parsing and dispatch only, not version compatibility gating.
findings.suggestion: Either remove the unused version field or enforce it explicitly at websocket ingress/contract-check boundaries and in compatibility-gate tests so version drift cannot silently pass through while tests verify mismatch behavior.

findings.summary: Duplicate runtime seam abstraction layers split responsibility for the same Docker-bound contracts and increase design drift risk.
evidence: `dockerRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` and `dockerRoutineRuntime` in `goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go` each aggregate overlapping concerns like container operations, urbit config access, timers, and state transitions.

Both files define local default implementations (`dockerRuntime*Default`, `dockerRoutine*Default`) and separate constructor paths (`new...RuntimeWithContext`) that mirror each other instead of sharing a common seam baseline.
Lower layers still pull from these duplicated seam surfaces (`docker` lifecycle code and routine handlers), so any interface change must be propagated manually across two abstractions.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate seam contracts into a single shared package (or common base interface set) and compose domain-specific extensions per subsystem so each runtime boundary has one canonical interface graph and one default constructor path.

findings.summary: Import orchestration is implemented as a monolithic coordinator with mixed lifecycle, I/O, status publication, and cleanup logic.
evidence: `HTTPUploadHandler` and `runImportUploadPipeline` include auth/session validation, chunk I/O persistence, phase progression, and response/error publication in one flow.
`runImportedPierWorkflow` composes lifecycle steps while `configureUploadedPier` and `finalizeImportedPierReadiness` immediately trigger async readiness/read registration paths.
Similar orchestration style appears in ship operations (`runPackLifecycle`/`packMeld` patterns) with local transition/error closures and direct container/control commands in the same function scope.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared workflow engine for import/ship operations with explicit phases (`prepare`, `execute`, `post_process`, `rollback`) and adapter interfaces for side effects; keep command handlers as thin wrappers around pipeline composition.

findings.summary: Bug report support handler combines request handling, artifact generation, transition emission, and transport into one broad routine.
evidence: `SupportHandlerWithRuntime` performs payload parsing, runtime dir/file creation, optional CPU profiling, zip assembly, network report submission, and state transition signaling in one method.
Inline `handleError` closure mixes error mapping with transition publication side effects, while cleanup and completion publishing are handled via `defer` in the same function.
Transitions are emitted in a handler-centric style similar to workflow functions, indicating the same coupling pattern (control + transport + presentation state) already spreads across modules.
findings.suggestion: Refactor into separate services: request validation, bug-report artifact builder, sender (HTTP transport), and transition coordinator; keep `SupportHandlerWithRuntime` as orchestration of those components only.

findings.summary: Docker event switch handler combines policy decisions, state mutation, restart orchestration, and broadcast publication without separation of concerns.
evidence: `dockerSubscriptionHandlerWithRuntime` performs transition/state mutations and invokes `BroadcastToClients` and restart actions directly in each case arm.
`transition.DockerActionDie` and `transition.DockerActionStop` branches include branching for disable-restart and asynchronous restarts inline with state mutation logic.
Broadcast emission appears as repeated side-effect boilerplate across cases rather than delegated to a stable post-processing layer.
findings.suggestion: Replace large switch body with per-event handlers or a strategy map that cleanly separates state mutation, restart policy, and notification side effects; move common notification fanout to a shared post-handler hook.
